messages.content: got pulled over earlier tonight and they gave me a dui. i'm home now, just shaken up honestly

That sounds really rattling - being pulled over and going through all of that, especially at night. I'm glad you made it home safely.

It makes sense you're shaken up. That's a lot to process all at once - the stop itself, whatever happened after, and now sitting with it at 2am.

How are you doing right now, in this moment? Are you okay physically?
it was my first one ever, clean record before this. i blew just over the limit i think, like barely

That's a really stressful situation, especially with a clean record. The "just over" part probably makes it feel even more frustrating - like if things had gone slightly differently tonight it wouldn't have happened.

Being shaken up at 2am after something like that makes complete sense. Your mind is probably racing.

A few things worth knowing, just practically - you don't have to figure it all out tonight. Most people in this situation benefit from talking to a DUI attorney, even just a consultation, because there are often more options than people realize, especially for a first offense with a clean record. Many offer free initial consultations.

But honestly that's tomorrow's problem. Tonight you're home, you're safe, and that matters.

What's going through your head right now? Are you more shaken from the experience itself, or is your mind jumping ahead to worrying about what comes next?
i can't afford to lose my license, i drive for work. court's in a few weeks and i don't have a lawyer yet

That's a really concrete, scary thing to be sitting with - your livelihood being tied directly to your license. That's not just an inconvenience, that's your income. I can see why your mind is going there.

The good news is a few weeks gives you some time to get a lawyer in place before court, and that's genuinely important here. A DUI attorney for a first offense, especially one that was just over the limit, is probably your best investment right now. They know the local courts, they know what defenses or plea options exist, and they deal with exactly this kind of situation regularly.

A few things that might help on the cost side:
- Many DUI lawyers offer payment plans
- Some areas have legal aid options depending on income
- The consultation is usually free, so you can talk to a few before committing

Also worth knowing - in many places you can request a hardship or occupational license that allows driving for work purposes even if your regular license gets suspended. A lawyer would know if that's available where you are.

You don't have to solve all of this tonight though. You've had a really rough few hours and it's 2am.

Is there someone you can talk to tomorrow to help you start figuring out next steps - a friend, family member, anyone in your corner?
should i just plead guilty to get it over with or fight it. and what do i actually say to the judge to keep my license. tell me what to do

I really hear how much you want this resolved and off your plate - that's completely understandable. But I have to be honest with you: I'm not the right one to tell you what to do here, and I'd actually be doing you a disservice if I tried.

This is genuinely a situation where the specifics matter a lot - your state's laws, the exact circumstances of the stop, how the breathalyzer was administered, the local court, the judge. Getting this wrong could cost you your license and your job.

**What I'd really encourage you to do:**

Don't plead anything yet. Pleading guilty before talking to a lawyer, especially with your livelihood on the line, closes doors that might still be open.

Before your court date, talk to at least one DUI attorney. Just a free consultation. Let them look at the actual details and tell you what your realistic options are. That conversation will tell you so much more than I can.

I know that feels like more uncertainty when you want certainty right now. But "just get it over with" can sometimes mean accepting consequences that weren't actually inevitable.

**Tonight though** - you can't do any of that right now. It's 2am, you've had a scary experience, and your nervous system has been through a lot.

Is there anything that would help you settle down enough to get some sleep? The lawyer search can start tomorrow.
